snippets.title: Shallow vs deep stack (conceptual layer count)
snippets.code:
# Conceptual: depth = number of learned transformation layers

shallow = ["input", "hidden", "output"]           # 1 hidden layer

deep = ["input", "h1", "h2", "h3", "h4", "output"]  # 4 hidden layers

print("shallow hidden layers:", len(shallow) - 2)
print("deep hidden layers:", len(deep) - 2)
snippets.explanation: Deep learning is not a single formula — it is characterized by stacking multiple learnable layers so representations build hierarchically.

snippets.title: Minimal deep classifier in PyTorch
snippets.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TinyDeepClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim=784, hidden=128, classes=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, classes),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

model = TinyDeepClassifier()
x = torch.randn(32, 784)  # batch of flattened images
logits = model(x)
print(logits.shape)  # torch.Size([32, 10])
snippets.explanation: Two hidden layers make this a small deep network: each Linear+ReLU block learns a new representation before the final class scores.

snippets.title: Representation view: activations per layer
snippets.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(4, 8), nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(8, 4), nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(4, 2),
)

x = torch.tensor([[1.0, 0.5, -0.2, 0.1]])
h1 = model[0:2](x)   # first representation
h2 = model[2:4](h1) # second representation
logits = model[4](h2)

print("h1 shape:", h1.shape, "h2 shape:", h2.shape)
snippets.explanation: Intermediate tensors h1 and h2 are learned representations — not hand-crafted features — that deeper layers consume.
